• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Phishing URL Detection - Hypothesis & EDA

library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggcorrplot)
library(yardstick)

1. Problem Statement

1.1 Real-world scenario

A user clicks a link in an email, chat, or search result. The corporate proxy has to decide before the page loads whether the link is phishing. The cheapest signal at that moment is the URL string itself - no DNS lookup, no HTML fetch, no rendering. If a URL-only check is good enough, we can block the link for free. If it isn’t, we have to fall back to a slower page-level check.

The question is : how much phishing signal is already in the URL? If a URL-only model works well enough, it can sit at the front as a fast first-line filter. Where it fails, the slower trust and behavior-level checks have to take over.

The proxy does not output a confidence score - it outputs a block/allow verdict. When a URL classifier produces a probability \(p \in [0, 1]\), the standard operating point is to use threshold 0.5: classify as phishing if \(p >= 0.5\), legitimate if \(p < 0.5\).

Explored scenarios for hypothesis driven modeling: 2,3,4

1.2 Dataset

The PhiUSIIL dataset has ~235 k rows and 50 predictor features. We split them into three families by how expensive they are to compute:

Family What it captures Example features
Lexical Properties of the URL string URLLength, NoOfSubDomain, NoOfObfuscatedChar, CharContinuationRate
Trust Domain / title consistency IsHTTPS, HasTitle, HasFavicon, Bank, Pay, Crypto
Behavior Page-level content signals NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfiFrame, HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields

2. Hypotheses

H1 (Scenario 2) - The gap between parametric and non-parametric model families is tier-dependent

Phishing URLs usually leave lexical traces: odd subdomain depth, paths full of digits, obfuscated characters. None of this needs the page to be downloaded. A Lexical-only model should still reach a reasonable AUC, but the size of the gap between parametric and non-parametric methods should depend on which tier we train on.

We compare three parametric methods (LR, LDA, Naive Bayes) against three non-parametric ones (RF, SVM-RBF, KNN).

Because our project frame is a corporate-proxy URL phishing detector , the deployment cost is a single block/allow verdict at threshold 0.5 per click. A model with high AUC but low Specificity creates false blocks, one with low Sensitivity quietly lets phish through. The H1 gating criteria therefore target the weakest-link operating-point metric \(\mathrm{minSS} = \min(\mathrm{Sensitivity}, \mathrm{Specificity})\) at the default 0.5 cut, with AUC kept as a complementary ranking criterion.

# Criterion Threshold
C1 Test-set \(\mathrm{minSS}\) gap between the best non-parametric and best parametric model on the Lexical (URLOnly) tier Δ minSS >= 0.10
C2 Gradient across tiers in deployment-relevant terms: \(\mathrm{minSS}\) gap is strictly larger on Lexical than on FullLite gap(Lexical) > gap(FullLite)
C3 Sanity check on ranking discrimination: mean test AUC gap on Lexical ΔAUC >= 0.02

H1 is supported only if C1 and C2 hold - both gated on the deployment metric (minSS at threshold 0.5). C3 is informative but not gating. It confirms the families also differ on the threshold-free metric, but a non-deployable model with a high AUC can’t satisfy the project goal regardless of how it ranks. H1 is rejected if either C1 or C2 fails.

Note: The FullLite tier is the replacement for a naive “Full” tier: six Behavior near-leakers are stripped because they push Full to AUC ≈ 1.0 and hide every contrast H1 is trying to measure.

H2 (Scenario 3) - A smaller URL-only filter can preserve operating quality

Scenario 3 compares feature-selection strategies: one algorithmic method (bidirectional stepAIC) and two embedded methods (lasso, elastic-net).

The primary tier is again Lexical, to follow on Scenario 2. FullLite stays as a fallback benchmark, if URL-only is not enough and the proxy must inspect Trust/Behavior signals too. Are we able to shrink the combined filter to a much smaller set without losing AUC, sensitivity, or specificity?

Scenario 3 H2: on the Lexical tier, at least one of the three feature-selection methods can produce a compact deployable URL filter with strong held-out operating quality.

# Criterion Threshold
C1 Sparsity on the lexical final fit at least one FS method keeps =< 9 / 13 predictors (>=30% reduction)
C2 Held-out ranking quality on the same 20% test set AUC >= 0.95
C3 Held-out operating point at threshold 0.5 Sensitivity >= 0.94 and Specificity >= 0.75

3. Data Loading & Cleaning

# Strip UTF-8 BOM that can appear on the first column name.
raw <- read_csv("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  rename_with(~ str_remove(.x, "^\uFEFF"))
# Columns excluded from modeling (identifiers / free text)
ID_COLS <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")

# Binary target as factor
df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label_bin = factor(label, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(ID_COLS))

# Feature matrix (numeric predictors only)
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("Observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
Observations: 235795 
cat("Predictors:  ", ncol(features), "\n")
Predictors:   50

Feature-family mapping

The three families (Lexical / Trust / Behavior) are our own taxonomy, not a property of the dataset. They reflect the cost of obtaining each signal at decision time: Lexical - derivable from the raw URL string alone. Trust - requires light page/domain inspection or an external lookup (DNS, TLD database, HTTPS handshake, title parsing). Behavior - requires fetching, parsing and partially rendering the page.

Note on IsHTTPS: although the “https://” scheme is part of the URL string, we place it in the Trust family. Modern browsers refuse plain HTTP for any sensitive interaction and warn the user prominently, so the scheme is already enforced by the trust layer of the browser rather than acting as a free lexical clue. Treating it as Lexical would let the URLOnly tier cheat off a binary that is, in practice, a deployment/trust signal.

family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                    ~family,
  "URLLength",                 "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",              "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                "Lexical",
  "URLSimilarityIndex",        "Trust",
  "CharContinuationRate",      "Lexical",
  "TLDLegitimateProb",         "Trust",
  "URLCharProb",               "Trust",
  "TLDLength",                 "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",             "Lexical",
  "HasObfuscation",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",        "Lexical",
  "ObfuscationRatio",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",          "Lexical",
  "LetterRatioInURL",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "DegitRatioInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",        "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL","Lexical",
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL",     "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                   "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",         "Behavior",
  "HasTitle",                  "Trust",
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",     "Trust",
  "URLTitleMatchScore",        "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                "Trust",
  "Robots",                    "Trust",
  "IsResponsive",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",          "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",     "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",              "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",           "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",           "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",          "Behavior",
  "Bank",                      "Trust",
  "Pay",                       "Trust",
  "Crypto",                    "Trust",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",          "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                   "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",           "Behavior"
)

3.3 Feature Exclusion

To start we drop three groups of features that would bias the analysis or trivialise the modeling. All three decisions are based on what the feature actually means.

Category 1 - Computed probability / similarity scores

These are not raw URL measurements. They are outputs of other models or expert-written scoring functions that already encode phishing-detection logic. Keeping them would mean we are classifying another classifier’s output, not the URL itself.

SCORES <- c(
  "URLSimilarityIndex",    # similarity to known-phishing URL patterns
  "TLDLegitimateProb",     # P(TLD is legitimate) from an external lookup
  "URLCharProb",           # P(character sequence) from a language model
  "DomainTitleMatchScore", # computed domain-title consistency score
  "URLTitleMatchScore"     # computed URL-title consistency score
)
cat("Computed scores to remove:", paste(SCORES, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Computed scores to remove: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore

Category 2 - Redundant binary indicator

HasObfuscation is literally (NoOfObfuscatedChar > 0). It holds no information that the count feature doesn’t already carry.

REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"
cat("Redundant binary to remove:", REDUNDANT_BINARY, "\n")
Redundant binary to remove: HasObfuscation

Category 3 - Redundant ratio features

Each ratio below equals count / URLLength, and both the count and URLLength are already in the dataset. Keeping the ratio gives us a perfect linear dependence and no extra signal.

RATIOS <- c(
  "LetterRatioInURL",      # = NoOfLettersInURL / URLLength
  "DegitRatioInURL",       # = NoOfDegitsInURL / URLLength
  "ObfuscationRatio",      # = NoOfObfuscatedChar / URLLength
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL"  # = NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL / URLLength
)
cat("Linearly dependent ratios to remove:", paste(RATIOS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Linearly dependent ratios to remove: LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL 
EXCLUDE_COLS <- c(SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df       <- df %>% select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS))
features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("\nFeatures after exclusion:", ncol(features), "\n")

Features after exclusion: 40 
cat("Removed:", paste(EXCLUDE_COLS, collapse = ", "), "\n")
Removed: URLSimilarityIndex, TLDLegitimateProb, URLCharProb, DomainTitleMatchScore, URLTitleMatchScore, HasObfuscation, LetterRatioInURL, DegitRatioInURL, ObfuscationRatio, SpacialCharRatioInURL

Update the family map to reflect the reduced feature set:

family_map <- family_map %>%
  filter(!feature %in% EXCLUDE_COLS)

family_map %>% count(family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  family       n
  <chr>    <int>
1 Behavior    20
2 Lexical     13
3 Trust        7

3.4 Feature Tiers Used in Modeling

tier_lexical  <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature)
tier_trust    <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature)
tier_behavior <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature)
tier_full     <- c(tier_lexical, tier_trust, tier_behavior)

cat("URLOnly tier (Lexical, ", length(tier_lexical),  " features)\n", sep = "")
URLOnly tier (Lexical, 13 features)
cat("Trust tier              (", length(tier_trust),    " features)\n", sep = "")
Trust tier              (7 features)
cat("Behavior tier           (", length(tier_behavior), " features)\n", sep = "")
Behavior tier           (20 features)
cat("Full tier               (", length(tier_full),     " features)\n", sep = "")
Full tier               (40 features)

4. Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Data Overview

4.1.1 Class Balance

df %>%
  count(label_bin) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, n, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct, 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .1))) +
  labs(title = "Class distribution", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

The dataset is roughly balanced, so we don’t need resampling or class-weighting.

4.1.2 Missing Values

No missing values are present in any predictor (verified with colSums(is.na(features))).

4.2 H1 preparation

H1 claims that a URL-only detector (Lexical features) will show a bigger parametric vs. non-parametric gap than any other tier. We don’t test non-linearity directly in the EDA. Instead we show that per-feature discriminative power is lowest in the Lexical family.

4.2.1 Discriminative Power by Family

We measure discriminative power separately for continuous and binary features. The standardised mean difference (SMD) only makes sense for continuous features. For binary features the “pooled SD” collapses to a function of the class proportion, so SMD stops being comparable across features. For those we use Cramér’s V instead - the standard effect-size measure for a 2×2 contingency table, on the same 0–1 scale as |correlation|.

# Continuous features: standardised mean difference.
smd_df <- df %>%
  select(label, all_of(continuous_features)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(feature, label) %>%
  summarise(m = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), s = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = c(m, s),
              names_glue = "{.value}_{label}") %>%
  transmute(
    feature,
    effect = abs(m_1 - m_0) / sqrt((s_0^2 + s_1^2) / 2 + 1e-9),
    metric = "SMD (continuous)"
  )

# Binary features: Cramér's V on a 2x2 contingency table.
cramers_v <- function(x, y) {
  tab <- table(x, y)
  if (any(dim(tab) < 2)) return(NA_real_)
  chi2 <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tab, correct = FALSE)$statistic)
  n <- sum(tab)
  as.numeric(sqrt(chi2 / (n * (min(dim(tab)) - 1))))
}

cv_df <- tibble(
  feature = binary_features,
  effect  = map_dbl(binary_features, ~ cramers_v(features[[.x]], df$label)),
  metric  = "Cramer's V (binary)"
)

discrim_df <- bind_rows(smd_df, cv_df) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(effect))

ggplot(discrim_df, aes(x = reorder(feature, effect), y = effect, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Per-feature discriminative power",
       x = NULL, y = "Effect size", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Effect-size distribution per family (continuous features only):

discrim_df %>%
  filter(metric == "SMD (continuous)", family != "Other") %>%
  ggplot(aes(family, effect, fill = family)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "SMD distribution by family (continuous features only)",
       x = NULL, y = "| SMD |") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Takeaway (H1): Among the continuous features, Lexical has the lowest median |SMD| and the smallest spread. Trust and Behavior carry more per-feature signal. This supports H1’s claim that the URL-only tier has less signal in individual features and relies more on feature combinations.

4.3 Collinearity inside Lexical

Lexical features (URLLength, NoOfLettersInURL, NoOfDegitsInURL) measure overlapping URL properties, causing severe collinearity. For linear models (LR, LDA), this makes coefficients numerically unstable. Unregularised LR fails here so we use Ridge regularisation to stabilise estimates. Trust and Behavior are mostly uncorrelated binary flags - no collinearity issue there.

4.3.1 Lexical Correlation Matrix

lexical_cor <- features %>%
  select(all_of(tier_lexical)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

suppressWarnings(
  ggcorrplot(
    lexical_cor,
    type     = "lower",
    hc.order = TRUE,
    lab      = TRUE,
    lab_size = 2.5,
    colors   = c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"),
    title    = "Correlation - Lexical features"
  )
)

Across the full feature matrix, 10 pairs pass |r| > 0.7, and all of them sit inside this Lexical count cluster.

4.3.2 Variance Inflation Factors

X_vif <- features %>% select(all_of(continuous_features))

# Regress each continuous feature on the remaining continuous features.
vif_df <- tibble(
  feature = names(X_vif),
  vif = map_dbl(names(X_vif), ~ {
    fmla <- reformulate(setdiff(names(X_vif), .x), response = .x)
    1 / (1 - summary(lm(fmla, data = X_vif))$r.squared)
  })
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(severity = case_when(
    vif > 10 ~ "severe (> 10)",
    vif >= 5 ~ "moderate (5-10)",
    TRUE     ~ "ok (< 5)"
  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(vif))

vif_df %>%
  transmute(Feature = feature,
            Family  = family,
            VIF     = round(vif, 2),
            Severity = severity) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Variance Inflation Factors - continuous features")
Variance Inflation Factors - continuous features
Feature Family VIF Severity
URLLength Lexical 1070.20 severe (> 10)
NoOfLettersInURL Lexical 535.40 severe (> 10)
NoOfDegitsInURL Lexical 90.60 severe (> 10)
NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL Lexical 10.16 severe (> 10)
NoOfEqualsInURL Lexical 7.94 moderate (5-10)
NoOfObfuscatedChar Lexical 4.90 ok (< 5)
NoOfAmpersandInURL Lexical 3.18 ok (< 5)
CharContinuationRate Lexical 2.77 ok (< 5)
NoOfSelfRef Behavior 2.19 ok (< 5)
NoOfExternalRef Behavior 1.98 ok (< 5)
NoOfSubDomain Lexical 1.95 ok (< 5)
NoOfQMarkInURL Lexical 1.86 ok (< 5)
DomainLength Lexical 1.80 ok (< 5)
LineOfCode Behavior 1.38 ok (< 5)
NoOfImage Behavior 1.34 ok (< 5)
NoOfJS Behavior 1.13 ok (< 5)
NoOfiFrame Behavior 1.11 ok (< 5)
TLDLength Lexical 1.11 ok (< 5)
NoOfCSS Behavior 1.08 ok (< 5)
NoOfEmptyRef Behavior 1.03 ok (< 5)
LargestLineLength Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)
NoOfPopup Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)

Takeaway: URLLength, NoOfLettersInURL and NoOfDegitsInURL stay near-collinear because they measure overlapping properties of the same URL string. Consequence for modeling:

Unpenalised LR is numerically unstable. Ridge (α = 0) is the minimal fix that bounds the coefficients without collapsing the cluster.

4.4 Skewness Assessment (Scenario 2 preparation)

# Skewness for all continuous features
skewness_fn <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  n <- length(x)
  m <- mean(x)
  s <- sd(x)
  (n / ((n-1)*(n-2))) * sum(((x - m) / s)^3)
}

# Apply the same skewness function to every continuous predictor.
skew_df <- features %>%
  select(all_of(continuous_features)) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), skewness_fn)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "feature", values_to = "skewness") %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(skewness)))

ggplot(skew_df, aes(reorder(feature, abs(skewness)), skewness, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Skewness of continuous features",
       x = NULL, y = "Skewness", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

cat("Features with |skewness| > 2:",
    sum(abs(skew_df$skewness) > 2), "of", nrow(skew_df), "\n")
Features with |skewness| > 2: 19 of 22 
cat("These are:\n")
These are:
skew_df %>% filter(abs(skewness) > 2) %>% select(feature, skewness, family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 19 × 3
   feature                    skewness family  
   <chr>                         <dbl> <chr>   
 1 NoOfCSS                      464.   Behavior
 2 NoOfObfuscatedChar           205.   Lexical 
 3 NoOfJS                       140.   Behavior
 4 NoOfEqualsInURL              115.   Lexical 
 5 NoOfEmptyRef                 107.   Behavior
 6 NoOfAmpersandInURL           107.   Lexical 
 7 NoOfiFrame                    97.7  Behavior
 8 NoOfDegitsInURL               94.9  Lexical 
 9 NoOfPopup                     85.0  Behavior
10 NoOfExternalRef               65.9  Behavior
11 NoOfSelfRef                   60.4  Behavior
12 NoOfLettersInURL              58.4  Lexical 
13 URLLength                     53.3  Lexical 
14 LineOfCode                    53.1  Behavior
15 LargestLineLength             48.2  Behavior
16 NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL    47.4  Lexical 
17 NoOfImage                     28.2  Behavior
18 NoOfQMarkInURL                 8.16 Lexical 
19 DomainLength                   2.51 Lexical

Takeaway: Several count features are heavily right-skewed with long outlier tails. This shapes our modeling choices. LR and LDA need scaling and a log-transform on skewed features for the distributional assumption to hold. Trees handle skew without preprocessing. KNN and SVM also tolerate skew, but they still need standardisation since they’re distance-based.

4.5 Near-leaker analysis (Critical for Scenario 2 tier design)

Six Behavior features are exceptionally discriminative on their own. To understand the scope of the problem for H1, we measure their univariate AUC - the discriminative power of each feature in isolation, before any model fitting:

behavior_feats <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature)
label_factor   <- factor(df$label, levels = c(0, 1))

univariate_aucs <- tibble(
  feature = behavior_feats,
  auc = map_dbl(behavior_feats, ~
    roc_auc_vec(
      truth       = label_factor,
      estimate    = features[[.x]],
      event_level = "second"
    )
  )
) %>%
  arrange(desc(auc))

LEAKY_BEHAVIOR <- univariate_aucs %>%
  filter(auc > 0.95) %>%
  pull(feature)

cat("Behavior features ranked by univariate AUC:\n")
Behavior features ranked by univariate AUC:
print(univariate_aucs, n = Inf)
# A tibble: 20 × 2
   feature                 auc
   <chr>                 <dbl>
 1 LineOfCode            0.990
 2 NoOfExternalRef       0.988
 3 NoOfImage             0.980
 4 NoOfSelfRef           0.972
 5 NoOfJS                0.971
 6 NoOfCSS               0.958
 7 HasSocialNet          0.895
 8 HasCopyrightInfo      0.875
 9 HasDescription        0.846
10 LargestLineLength     0.816
11 HasSubmitButton       0.788
12 IsResponsive          0.768
13 NoOfiFrame            0.755
14 HasHiddenFields       0.749
15 NoOfEmptyRef          0.745
16 NoOfPopup             0.545
17 HasPasswordField      0.542
18 HasExternalFormSubmit 0.535
19 NoOfSelfRedirect      0.485
20 NoOfURLRedirect       0.484
cat("\nNear-leakers (univariate AUC > 0.95):\n")

Near-leakers (univariate AUC > 0.95):
cat(paste(LEAKY_BEHAVIOR, collapse = ", "), "\n")
LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS

The saturation problem. Phishing pages are usually shallow HTML shells with minimal content, so the six near-leakers-LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS-are nearly perfect phishing signals on their own:

Feature Univariate AUC Why it’s near-perfect
LineOfCode >0.99 Legit sites are complex
NoOfExternalRef 0.990 Phish don’t reference external resources
NoOfImage 0.985 Phish pages use almost no images
NoOfSelfRef 0.980 Legit sites self-reference heavily
NoOfJS 0.970 Phish avoid JavaScript
NoOfCSS 0.958 Phish minimize stylesheets

If we include these six in any tier, any model, parametric or non-parametric reaches AUC ≈ 0.99+ because the signal is so strong, even a simple threshold on a single feature nearly solves the task. —

5. EDA Summary & Modeling Implications

After the Section 3.3 exclusions, 40 features remain (13 Lexical + 7 Trust + 20 Behavior). On this reduced set the task is near-perfectly separable as soon as Behavior is included. Six Behavior counts have univariate AUC > 0.95 on their own, so a naive Full tier saturates at AUC ≈ 1.0 and the parametric vs. non-parametric contrast just disappears.

For H1 we therefore use the scenario_2 family layout: Lexical (13), Trust (7), Behavior with the six near-leakers stripped (14), and FullLite (34) = Lexical + Trust + reduced Behavior. H1 is tested primarily on the URLOnly tier, where the family contrast has the most room to show, and the family gradient (C2) is measured against FullLite, not Full.

5.1 Implications for modeling scenarios

Finding Scenario 2 implication (H1) Additional EDA implication
Lexical SMD lowest + features must be combined Largest parametric/non-parametric AUC gap expected on URLOnly Feature interactions matter more than single-feature thresholds in URL-only detection
Heavy skewness in 19 of 22 continuous features Log-transform + standardisation for LR/LDA; trees/RF unaffected Keep one consistent preprocessing recipe across model families for fair comparisons
